module: Module 4: Solving One-Step and Two-Step Equations

sections.text: Hey, welcome back. Algebra One, Module Four — solving one-step and two-step equations. If you've ever counted change at a bubble tea shop, you've already done this. Today we just give it a name.

Quick story. You walk up to the counter. Large milk tea, two hundred New Taiwan dollars. You hand over a five hundred. Without thinking, your brain goes — three hundred. That right there? Solving an equation. Two hundred plus x equals five hundred. Find x. You've been doing algebra since you were a kid. You just didn't call it that. So when you see two x plus three equals eleven later in this lesson and your brain wants to nope out — don't let it. You've got this.

Here's what we'll do. First, we'll talk about why an equation is like a balance scale. Second, we'll learn one move — inverse operations. Third, we'll knock out one-step equations like they owe you money. Then two-step equations. And we'll always check the answer because honestly, half of math mistakes are silly arithmetic, and checking takes ten seconds.

An equation is a balance scale. Whatever sits on the left side has the exact same value as whatever sits on the right side. Our job? Find what x is, without tipping the scale. The rule is simple, and I want you to write this down because it's the only rule for the whole lesson. Whatever you do to one side, you do to the other side. That's it. That's the rule. Break it and the scale tips and your answer's wrong.

To get x by itself, we use inverse operations. An inverse just means an operation that undoes another. Adding undoes subtracting. Multiplying undoes dividing. So if a five is being added to your variable, you subtract a five. If your variable is being multiplied by three, you divide by three. We use the inverse to peel off whatever's stuck to x, layer by layer, until x is alone. That's the whole game.

Example one. x plus five equals twelve. What's stuck to x? A plus five. Inverse of plus five is minus five. So we subtract five from both sides — both sides, remember the rule. Plus five minus five cancels out on the left. Twelve minus five is seven on the right. x equals seven. One move. Done. Next.

Example two. Three x equals fifteen. Three x means three times x. The three is multiplied. Inverse of multiplying by three is dividing by three. Both sides get divided by three. The threes cancel on the left. Fifteen over three is five. x equals five. Notice the pattern? Identify what's attached to x, then apply the inverse. That's literally it.
Okay — your turn. x minus seven equals ten. Pause the video, solve it, come back. I'll wait.

Time's up. Did you get x equals seventeen? You added seven to both sides — minus seven plus seven cancels, ten plus seven is seventeen. If you got it, nice work. If you didn't — rewind, do it again, it'll click. Math is mostly reps, not magic.

Now — two-step equations. These look scarier but they're just two one-step equations stacked. There's only one new thing to remember. The order. When you have plus or minus AND multiply or divide on the same side, you undo plus or minus first, multiply or divide second. Why? Because we're reversing the order of operations. PEMDAS in reverse. That's it.

Example three. Two x plus three equals eleven. Two things are stuck to x — a times two, and a plus three. Plus three goes first. Subtract three from both sides. Eleven minus three is eight. Now we've got two x equals eight — that's just a one-step equation. Divide both sides by two. Eight over two is four. x equals four. See? Two one-step problems pretending to be one big problem.
Round two. Try this one. Five x minus two equals thirteen. Pause, solve, come back.

Welcome back. The answer is x equals three. Step one — add two to both sides, you get five x equals fifteen. Step two — divide both sides by five, x equals three. If you got it — you're officially fluent in two-step equations, and we're not even halfway through the module.

One more, just to make sure. x divided by four, minus two, equals five. The x is being divided by four AND minus two is hanging on. Plus or minus first — add two to both sides. Five plus two is seven. Now we have x over four equals seven. Multiply both sides by four. Seven times four is twenty-eight. x equals twenty-eight. Smooth.

Check it. Always. Take twenty-eight, plug it back into the original. Twenty-eight divided by four is seven. Seven minus two is five. Five equals five. Balanced. We're good. This step takes ten seconds and saves you from getting half-credit on a problem you actually understood.

Recap. Equation equals balance. Inverse operations isolate the variable. Two-step equations? Plus or minus first, multiply or divide second. Always check by plugging back in. Your assignment is in the dashboard — five practice problems and three word problems. Smash them out, and I'll see you in Module Five, where we get spicy and put variables on both sides. Good work today.
